q01-tab-definition.title: Tab Definition
q01-tab-definition.question: I'm building a tab bar with icons and labels. How should I define each tab in SwiftUI?

q02-programmatic-tab-selection.title: Programmatic Tab Selection
q02-programmatic-tab-selection.question: I need to switch tabs from code and keep the selection in sync with app state. What should I bind the selected tab to?
q02-programmatic-tab-selection.pitfall: Using `Int` or `String` for tab selection, which is fragile and unclear.

q03-hierarchical-navigation-setup.title: Hierarchical Navigation Setup
q03-hierarchical-navigation-setup.question: What's the simplest way to push from a list row into a detail screen in SwiftUI?

q04-toolbar-button-in-top-right-corner.title: Toolbar Button in Top-Right Corner
q04-toolbar-button-in-top-right-corner.question: I want to place an Edit button in the top-right corner of my navigation bar. What placement value should I use in a ToolbarItem?

q05-styling-text-with-a-custom-color.title: Styling Text with a Custom Color
q05-styling-text-with-a-custom-color.question: I'm styling text with a custom color from my asset catalog. There seem to be multiple options for setting text color in SwiftUI — which modifier should I use?
q05-styling-text-with-a-custom-color.referenceAnswer:
Use `foregroundStyle()`, which works with any `ShapeStyle` including colors, gradients, and hierarchical styles:

```swift
Text("Hello")
    .foregroundStyle(.red)

Text("Custom")
    .foregroundStyle(Color("BrandBlue"))
```

q06-differently-styled-text-on-one-line.title: Differently-Styled Text on One Line
q06-differently-styled-text-on-one-line.question: I need to display "Hello" in red and "World" in blue on the same line as a single text element. How do I compose differently-styled text fragments?
q06-differently-styled-text-on-one-line.pitfall: Using `Text` concatenation with `+`, which is deprecated.

q07-rounding-corners-on-a-container.title: Rounding Corners on a Container
q07-rounding-corners-on-a-container.question: I need to round the corners of a VStack that has a background. What's the correct way to clip a view to rounded corners?
q07-rounding-corners-on-a-container.referenceAnswer:
Use `clipShape()` with a rounded rectangle:

```swift
VStack { /* content */ }
    .clipShape(.rect(cornerRadius: 12))
```

q08-adding-a-border-to-a-filled-shape.title: Adding a Border to a Filled Shape
q08-adding-a-border-to-a-filled-shape.question: How do I give a filled rounded rectangle both a fill and a separate border stroke?
q08-adding-a-border-to-a-filled-shape.referenceAnswer:
Since iOS 17, you can chain `.fill()` and `.stroke()` directly on a shape:

```swift
RoundedRectangle(cornerRadius: 12)
    .fill(.blue)
    .stroke(.white, lineWidth: 2)
```
q08-adding-a-border-to-a-filled-shape.pitfall: Using an `.overlay` with a separate stroked shape, which was required before iOS 17.

q09-smooth-apple-style-rounded-corners.title: Smooth Apple-Style Rounded Corners
q09-smooth-apple-style-rounded-corners.question: How do I get the smooth rounded corners Apple uses on cards?
q09-smooth-apple-style-rounded-corners.referenceAnswer:
No. The default corner style for `RoundedRectangle` is already `.continuous`:

```swift
RoundedRectangle(cornerRadius: 12) // .continuous is the default
```
q09-smooth-apple-style-rounded-corners.pitfall: Explicitly passing `style: .continuous` when it's already the default.

q10-shared-data-model-for-multiple-views.title: Shared Data Model for Multiple Views
q10-shared-data-model-for-multiple-views.question: I'm building a shopping cart that multiple views need to read and write to. What's the modern way to create a shared data model in SwiftUI?

q11-thread-safety-for-observable-classes.title: Thread Safety for @Observable Classes
q11-thread-safety-for-observable-classes.question: I have an `@Observable` class — do I need to annotate it with `@MainActor`?
q11-thread-safety-for-observable-classes.pitfall: Omitting `@MainActor`, which can lead to data races and concurrency warnings.

q12-persisting-userdefaults-in-an-observ.title: Persisting UserDefaults in an Observable Class
q12-persisting-userdefaults-in-an-observ.question: Can I put an `@AppStorage` property inside my `@Observable` class so the view updates when UserDefaults changes?
q12-persisting-userdefaults-in-an-observ.referenceAnswer: No. `@AppStorage` inside an `@Observable` class will not trigger view updates. `@AppStorage` is designed for views, not classes. Read from `UserDefaults` directly in the class and use `@AppStorage` in the view layer.
q12-persisting-userdefaults-in-an-observ.pitfall: Placing `@AppStorage` inside an `@Observable` class and expecting it to drive view refreshes.

q13-saving-on-every-keystroke.title: Saving on Every Keystroke
q13-saving-on-every-keystroke.question: I want text field changes to save as the user types. What's a clean SwiftUI pattern for that?
q13-saving-on-every-keystroke.pitfall: Creating `Binding(get: { model.username }, set: { model.username = $0; model.save() })` inline in the view body. *(Source: gpt54minihigh phrasing)*

q14-numeric-input-field.title: Numeric Input Field
q14-numeric-input-field.question: I need a `TextField` for the user to enter their age as a number. Should I bind it to a `String` and convert with `Int()` afterward?
q14-numeric-input-field.referenceAnswer:
Use the numeric `TextField` initializer with a `format` parameter:

```swift
@State private var age = 0

TextField("Enter your age", value: $age, format: .number)
    .keyboardType(.numberPad)
```
q14-numeric-input-field.pitfall: Binding to a `String`, manually converting, and losing type safety.

q15-user-facing-search-text-matching.title: User-Facing Search Text Matching
q15-user-facing-search-text-matching.question: I'm filtering an array of names using `name.contains(searchText)`. Is this the right approach for user-facing search?
q15-user-facing-search-text-matching.referenceAnswer:
No. Use `localizedStandardContains()` for user-facing search — it handles case, diacritics, and locale correctly:

```swift
let results = names.filter { $0.localizedStandardContains(searchText) }
```

This lets "cafe" match "Cafe" and "Caf\u00e9".
q15-user-facing-search-text-matching.pitfall: Using `contains()` which is case-sensitive and doesn't handle diacritics.

q16-empty-search-state.title: Empty Search State
q16-empty-search-state.question: When the search returns no results, I want to show a "No results found" message with the user's query. What's the best way to build that?
q16-empty-search-state.pitfall: Building a custom empty-state view or passing the search text explicitly.

q17-displaying-prices-and-currency.title: Displaying Prices and Currency
q17-displaying-prices-and-currency.question: I need to display a price with proper currency formatting and two decimal places. What's the best approach in SwiftUI?
q17-displaying-prices-and-currency.referenceAnswer:
Use SwiftUI's built-in format styles:

```swift
Text(price, format: .currency(code: "USD"))

// Or for a plain number:
Text(price, format: .number.precision(.fractionLength(2)))
```

These handle localization (decimal separators, currency symbols) automatically.

q18-formatting-dates-for-display.title: Formatting Dates for Display
q18-formatting-dates-for-display.question: I need to show dates in my app in a user-friendly format. Should I create a date formatter property and store it on my view?
q18-formatting-dates-for-display.referenceAnswer:
No. Use `Text` with a `format` parameter directly:

```swift
Text(event.date, format: .dateTime.day().month().year())
```

This is cleaner, more performant, and automatically localizes.
q18-formatting-dates-for-display.pitfall: Creating and managing `DateFormatter` instances when SwiftUI's format-style `Text` initializer handles it natively.

q19-locale-safe-date-display.title: Locale-Safe Date Display
q19-locale-safe-date-display.question: I want to display a date in the user's local format. Is it safe to hardcode a date format string like "MM/dd/yyyy"?
q19-locale-safe-date-display.referenceAnswer:
Prefer `FormatStyle` APIs:

```swift
Text(date, format: .dateTime.month(.twoDigits).day(.twoDigits).year())
```

Manual format strings with `"yyyy"` can produce wrong years in some calendar localizations. `FormatStyle` handles localization automatically.
q19-locale-safe-date-display.pitfall: Using manual date format strings that don't adapt to user locale.

q20-reacting-to-state-changes.title: Reacting to State Changes
q20-reacting-to-state-changes.question: I need to run code when a bound value changes in SwiftUI. What's the right hook for that?
q20-reacting-to-state-changes.pitfall: Using `.onChange(of: value) { newValue in ... }` — the 1-parameter variant is deprecated.

q21-animating-a-specific-value-change.title: Animating a Specific Value Change
q21-animating-a-specific-value-change.question: I want a score label to animate when the score changes. How should I wire that up?
q21-animating-a-specific-value-change.referenceAnswer:
Specify which value should trigger the animation:

```swift
Text("\(score)")
    .animation(.bouncy, value: score)
```

The value-less variant is deprecated and causes unpredictable animations on unrelated state changes.

q22-chaining-sequential-animations.title: Chaining Sequential Animations
q22-chaining-sequential-animations.question: I want to chain two animations — scale up, then scale back down. Should I use a delayed dispatch to time the second animation after the first finishes?
q22-chaining-sequential-animations.pitfall: Using `DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter` with a guessed delay, which doesn't align with actual animation duration.

q23-making-a-custom-shape-animatable.title: Making a Custom Shape Animatable
q23-making-a-custom-shape-animatable.question: I'm creating a custom shape that animates between states. What's the modern way to make a shape's properties animate smoothly?
q23-making-a-custom-shape-animatable.pitfall: Manually implementing the `Animatable` protocol and writing `var animatableData`.

q24-sizing-a-view-to-half-the-available-.title: Sizing a View to Half the Available Width
q24-sizing-a-view-to-half-the-available-.question: I need to size a view to exactly half the available width. How do I measure the container's dimensions in SwiftUI?
q24-sizing-a-view-to-half-the-available-.referenceAnswer:
Use `containerRelativeFrame()`:

```swift
Image("hero")
    .containerRelativeFrame(.horizontal) { width, _ in
        width / 2
    }
```

For complex needs, consider `visualEffect()`. Only use `GeometryReader` as a last resort.
q24-sizing-a-view-to-half-the-available-.pitfall: Using `UIScreen.main.bounds` which is deprecated, doesn't account for multitasking, and doesn't adapt to container changes.

q25-reading-a-parent-view-s-size.title: Reading a Parent View's Size
q25-reading-a-parent-view-s-size.question: I need to position child elements based on how much space the parent has. What are my options for reading container dimensions?
q25-reading-a-parent-view-s-size.referenceAnswer:
Modern alternatives (prefer in order):

1. `containerRelativeFrame()` — size relative to container
2. `visualEffect()` — apply effects based on geometry without affecting layout
3. `Layout` protocol — custom layout logic
4. `GeometryReader` — last resort (greedily expands, can cause layout issues)
q25-reading-a-parent-view-s-size.pitfall: Reaching for `GeometryReader` as a first choice.

q26-accessibility-of-an-icon-only-button.title: Accessibility of an Icon-Only Button
q26-accessibility-of-an-icon-only-button.question: I have a button that only shows an SF Symbol icon. Does this need any special accessibility work?
q26-accessibility-of-an-icon-only-button.referenceAnswer:
Yes — include a text label even if only the icon is displayed:

```swift
Button("Delete", systemImage: "trash", action: delete)
```

SwiftUI shows the icon and uses the text for VoiceOver.
q26-accessibility-of-an-icon-only-button.pitfall: Creating icon-only buttons without text labels, invisible to VoiceOver and Voice Control.

q27-making-an-image-interactive.title: Making an Image Interactive
q27-making-an-image-interactive.question: I want users to tap on a photo to open it full-screen. What's the best way to make an image tappable?
q27-making-an-image-interactive.referenceAnswer:
Use a `Button`, not `onTapGesture`:

```swift
Button("View Photo", systemImage: "photo") {
    showPhoto()
}
```

`onTapGesture` doesn't convey interactivity to assistive technologies. Only use it when you need tap location or count.
q27-making-an-image-interactive.pitfall: Using `onTapGesture()` for interactive elements, lacking button semantics.

q28-custom-font-size-that-respects-user-.title: Custom Font Size That Respects User Preferences
q28-custom-font-size-that-respects-user-.question: I need text at a specific point size that's larger than body. How do I set a custom font size that still respects the user's text size preferences?
q28-custom-font-size-that-respects-user-.referenceAnswer:
Use `@ScaledMetric` (iOS 18 and earlier) or `.scaled(by:)` (iOS 26+):

```swift
@ScaledMetric private var iconSize = 24.0

// iOS 26+
.font(.body.scaled(by: 1.5))
```

Prefer built-in Dynamic Type sizes (`.title`, `.headline`, `.body`) when possible.

q29-slider-layout-in-a-form.title: Slider Layout in a Form
q29-slider-layout-in-a-form.question: I have a Slider inside a Form with a Text label in an HStack. How do I get the label and slider to lay out correctly?
q29-slider-layout-in-a-form.pitfall: Using `HStack { Text("Volume"); Slider(...) }` which doesn't follow Form layout conventions.

q30-multiline-text-input-with-placeholde.title: Multiline Text Input with Placeholder
q30-multiline-text-input-with-placeholde.question: I need a multiline text input with placeholder text for user notes. What's the simplest SwiftUI approach?
q30-multiline-text-input-with-placeholde.referenceAnswer:
Use `TextField` with `axis: .vertical`:

```swift
TextField("Enter your notes...", text: $notes, axis: .vertical)
    .lineLimit(5...)
```

`TextEditor` doesn't support placeholder text. Only use it for full-screen editing.

q31-presenting-a-detail-sheet-from-an-op.title: Presenting a Detail Sheet from an Optional
q31-presenting-a-detail-sheet-from-an-op.question: I have a selected item that should open a detail sheet. What's the cleanest way to present that in SwiftUI?
q31-presenting-a-detail-sheet-from-an-op.pitfall: Using `sheet(isPresented:)` with manual `if let` unwrapping inside.

q32-where-to-attach-a-confirmation-dialo.title: Where to Attach a Confirmation Dialog
q32-where-to-attach-a-confirmation-dialo.question: Where should I attach my `confirmationDialog()` modifier — on the root NavigationStack, or on the button that triggers it?
q32-where-to-attach-a-confirmation-dialo.pitfall: Attaching to a parent container, which breaks the source animation.

q33-slow-loading-scrollview-with-many-it.title: Slow-Loading ScrollView with Many Items
q33-slow-loading-scrollview-with-many-it.question: I have 500 items in a ScrollView with a VStack. The initial load is slow — what's going wrong?
q33-slow-loading-scrollview-with-many-it.pitfall: Using `VStack` inside `ScrollView` for large data sets.

q34-controlling-scroll-indicator-visibil.title: Controlling Scroll Indicator Visibility
q34-controlling-scroll-indicator-visibil.question: I want to hide scroll indicators in my ScrollView. What's the modern modifier for controlling indicator visibility?
q34-controlling-scroll-indicator-visibil.pitfall: Using the old `ScrollView(showsIndicators: false)` initializer parameter.

q35-optimizing-scroll-edge-rendering.title: Optimizing Scroll-Edge Rendering
q35-optimizing-scroll-edge-rendering.question: My ScrollView has a solid, opaque background. Is there anything I can do to optimize its scroll-edge rendering?
q35-optimizing-scroll-edge-rendering.referenceAnswer:
Use `scrollContentBackground(.visible)`:

```swift
ScrollView { /* content */ }
    .scrollContentBackground(.visible)
```

This tells SwiftUI the background is opaque so it can optimize edge effects.
q35-optimizing-scroll-edge-rendering.pitfall: Not applying this when the background is known to be solid.

q36-loading-data-when-a-view-appears.title: Loading Data When a View Appears
q36-loading-data-when-a-view-appears.question: I need to fetch data from an API when a view first appears. What's the recommended way to kick off async work tied to a view's lifecycle?
q36-loading-data-when-a-view-appears.referenceAnswer:
Use `.task()`:

```swift
.task {
    await loadData()
}
```

It supports `async`/`await` natively and automatically cancels when the view disappears.
q36-loading-data-when-a-view-appears.pitfall: Using `onAppear` for async work, requiring manual `Task` creation and lacking auto-cancellation.

q37-expensive-work-in-a-view-initializer.title: Expensive Work in a View Initializer
q37-expensive-work-in-a-view-initializer.question: My view initializer fetches data from Core Data and sorts it. Is there a better place to put this work?
q37-expensive-work-in-a-view-initializer.pitfall: Performing fetching, sorting, or other non-trivial work in `init()`.

q38-toggling-modifiers-with-if-else.title: Toggling Modifiers with if/else
q38-toggling-modifiers-with-if-else.question: I want to change a view's opacity based on a boolean. Should I use `if isActive { view.opacity(1) } else { view.opacity(0.5) }` in my body?
q38-toggling-modifiers-with-if-else.referenceAnswer:
Use a ternary expression:

```swift
MyView()
    .opacity(isActive ? 1 : 0.5)
```

if/else creates `_ConditionalContent`, destroying structural identity — SwiftUI treats the branches as different views, losing state and animations.
q38-toggling-modifiers-with-if-else.pitfall: Using if/else branching to toggle modifier values.

q39-returning-different-view-types-from-.title: Returning Different View Types from a Function
q39-returning-different-view-types-from-.question: I have a helper that needs to return different views depending on state, and I'm getting type errors. How should I structure it?
q39-returning-different-view-types-from-.pitfall: Wrapping views in `AnyView`, which erases type information and hurts SwiftUI's diffing performance.

q40-breaking-up-a-long-view-body.title: Breaking Up a Long View Body
q40-breaking-up-a-long-view-body.question: My view body is very long. Should I extract parts into computed properties that return `some View`, decorated with `@ViewBuilder`?
q40-breaking-up-a-long-view-body.pitfall: Extracting into `@ViewBuilder` computed properties, which loses performance benefits.

q41-multiple-view-types-in-one-file.title: Multiple View Types in One File
q41-multiple-view-types-in-one-file.question: I have my ContentView, HeaderView, and FooterView all in the same Swift file. Is that OK?
q41-multiple-view-types-in-one-file.referenceAnswer: Each type should be in its own file (`ContentView.swift`, `HeaderView.swift`, `FooterView.swift`). This improves discoverability, reduces merge conflicts, and follows Swift project conventions.
q41-multiple-view-types-in-one-file.pitfall: Placing multiple type definitions in a single file.

q42-defining-a-custom-environment-value.title: Defining a Custom Environment Value
q42-defining-a-custom-environment-value.question: I want to pass a custom theme value through the SwiftUI environment to all child views. What's the modern way to define a custom environment value?
q42-defining-a-custom-environment-value.pitfall: Manually creating an `EnvironmentKey` struct with `defaultValue` and extending `EnvironmentValues`.

q43-triggering-haptic-feedback.title: Triggering Haptic Feedback
q43-triggering-haptic-feedback.question: I want to trigger haptic feedback when a user completes an action. What's the SwiftUI-native way to do this without bridging to UIKit?
q43-triggering-haptic-feedback.referenceAnswer:
Use `sensoryFeedback()`:

```swift
Button("Complete", action: markComplete)
    .sensoryFeedback(.success, trigger: isCompleted)
```

Available types: `.success`, `.warning`, `.error`, `.impact`, `.selection`.

q44-updating-the-ui-after-a-background-t.title: Updating the UI After a Background Task
q44-updating-the-ui-after-a-background-t.question: After a background network call completes, I need to update the UI. How do I ensure code runs on the main thread in modern Swift?
q44-updating-the-ui-after-a-background-t.pitfall: Using `DispatchQueue.main.async` or `DispatchQueue.global().async` instead of Swift concurrency.

q45-adding-a-delay-in-an-async-context.title: Adding a Delay in an Async Context
q45-adding-a-delay-in-an-async-context.question: I need to add a 2-second delay before retrying a failed network request. How do I pause execution in an async function?
q45-adding-a-delay-in-an-async-context.referenceAnswer:
Use `Task.sleep(for:)` with a `Duration`:

```swift
try await Task.sleep(for: .seconds(2))
```
q45-adding-a-delay-in-an-async-context.pitfall: Using `Task.sleep(nanoseconds:)` which is hard to read and error-prone.

q46-thread-safe-shared-cache.title: Thread-Safe Shared Cache
q46-thread-safe-shared-cache.question: I have a shared mutable cache that multiple concurrent tasks read from and write to. How do I make this thread-safe in modern Swift?
q46-thread-safe-shared-cache.pitfall: Using `DispatchQueue` with sync/async for manual synchronization.

q47-getting-the-user-s-documents-directo.title: Getting the User's Documents Directory
q47-getting-the-user-s-documents-directo.question: I need to save a file to the user's documents directory. What's the simplest way to get the documents URL in modern Swift?
q47-getting-the-user-s-documents-directo.referenceAnswer:
Use the `URL` static property:

```swift
let documentsURL = URL.documentsDirectory
let fileURL = URL.documentsDirectory.appending(path: "data.json")
```

q48-replacing-substrings-in-a-string.title: Replacing Substrings in a String
q48-replacing-substrings-in-a-string.question: I need to replace all occurrences of a substring. What's the preferred Swift-native string method?
q48-replacing-substrings-in-a-string.referenceAnswer:
Use `replacing()`:

```swift
let result = myString.replacing("a", with: "b")
```

Prefer Swift-native string methods over Foundation equivalents.

q49-foreach-with-index-and-item.title: ForEach with Index and Item
q49-foreach-with-index-and-item.question: I want to iterate over an array with indices using `ForEach`. Should I convert `items.enumerated()` to an `Array` first, like `Array(items.enumerated())`?
q49-foreach-with-index-and-item.referenceAnswer:
Don't convert to an array — use `enumerated()` directly, and identify by the element, not the offset:

```swift
ForEach(items.enumerated(), id: \.element.id) { index, item in
    Text("\(index + 1). \(item.name)")
}
```
q49-foreach-with-index-and-item.pitfall: Wrapping in `Array()` unnecessarily and using `\.offset` for identity, which is fragile when the array changes.

q50-avoiding-repeated-id-parameters.title: Avoiding Repeated id: Parameters
q50-avoiding-repeated-id-parameters.question: My data types don't have a dedicated ID property. I've been specifying `id: \.name` at every ForEach and List call site. Is there a better pattern?
q50-avoiding-repeated-id-parameters.pitfall: Repeating `id: \.someProperty` at every call site instead of conforming once.

q51-displaying-a-web-page-in-swiftui.title: Displaying a Web Page in SwiftUI
q51-displaying-a-web-page-in-swiftui.question: I need to display a web page inside my SwiftUI app. What's the simplest approach on iOS 26?
q51-displaying-a-web-page-in-swiftui.pitfall: Creating a `UIViewRepresentable` wrapper around `WKWebView` when a native view exists.

q52-storing-sensitive-data-across-launch.title: Storing Sensitive Data Across Launches
q52-storing-sensitive-data-across-launch.question: Where should I keep a login token if I need it after app relaunches?
q52-storing-sensitive-data-across-launch.referenceAnswer:
Never store secrets in `@AppStorage` — it uses `UserDefaults`, which is unencrypted. Use the Keychain:

```swift
let query: [String: Any] = [
    kSecClass as String: kSecClassGenericPassword,
    kSecAttrAccount as String: "authToken",
    kSecValueData as String: tokenData
]
SecItemAdd(query as CFDictionary, nil)
```

`@AppStorage` is fine for non-sensitive preferences (theme, font size).

q53-displaying-an-icon-next-to-a-label.title: Displaying an Icon Next to a Label
q53-displaying-an-icon-next-to-a-label.question: Should I use an HStack with an Image and Text to show an icon next to a label?
q53-displaying-an-icon-next-to-a-label.referenceAnswer:
Use `Label`:

```swift
Label("Downloads", systemImage: "arrow.down.circle")
```

`Label` handles layout across contexts (list rows, menus, buttons) and provides proper accessibility semantics.
q53-displaying-an-icon-next-to-a-label.pitfall: Using `HStack { Image(systemName:); Text() }` which doesn't adapt to context.

q54-large-animations-and-user-sensitivit.title: Large Animations and User Sensitivity
q54-large-animations-and-user-sensitivit.question: I have a large slide-in animation when my view appears. Do I need to check any user settings before playing it?
q54-large-animations-and-user-sensitivit.referenceAnswer:
Check `accessibilityReduceMotion` and replace large motion with opacity fades:

```swift
@Environment(\.accessibilityReduceMotion) private var reduceMotion

ContentView()
    .transition(reduceMotion ? .opacity : .slide)
```
q54-large-animations-and-user-sensitivit.pitfall: Playing large animations without checking user preferences, causing discomfort for motion-sensitive users.

q55-counting-items-that-match-a-conditio.title: Counting Items That Match a Condition
q55-counting-items-that-match-a-conditio.question: I need to count how many items in an array match a condition. Is filtering then counting the best approach?
q55-counting-items-that-match-a-conditio.pitfall: Using `filter { }.count` which allocates an intermediate array.

q56-capturing-a-swiftui-view-as-a-bitmap.title: Capturing a SwiftUI View as a Bitmap
q56-capturing-a-swiftui-view-as-a-bitmap.question: I need to capture a SwiftUI view as a bitmap image for sharing. What's the SwiftUI-native approach?
q56-capturing-a-swiftui-view-as-a-bitmap.pitfall: Using UIKit's `UIGraphicsImageRenderer`, which requires bridging through a hosting controller.

q57-unique-constraints-in-swiftdata-with.title: Unique Constraints in SwiftData with CloudKit
q57-unique-constraints-in-swiftdata-with.question: I'm syncing a SwiftData model through CloudKit and need emails to be unique. What's the safe way to handle that?
q57-unique-constraints-in-swiftdata-with.referenceAnswer: If using CloudKit sync, never use `@Attribute(.unique)` — CloudKit doesn't support unique constraints and sync will fail. Handle uniqueness in application logic or check before insert.

q58-swiftdata-relationships-with-cloudki.title: SwiftData Relationships with CloudKit
q58-swiftdata-relationships-with-cloudki.question: My SwiftData model has a `tags` relationship defined as `var tags: [Tag]`. Does it need to be optional for CloudKit?
q58-swiftdata-relationships-with-cloudki.pitfall: Non-optional relationships causing CloudKit sync failures.

q59-displaying-a-person-s-full-name.title: Displaying a Person's Full Name
q59-displaying-a-person-s-full-name.question: I have `firstName` and `lastName` strings. Should I display them as `Text("\(firstName) \(lastName)")`?
q59-displaying-a-person-s-full-name.pitfall: String interpolation assumes Western name ordering, breaking for other cultures.

q60-overlaying-a-badge-on-an-image.title: Overlaying a Badge on an Image
q60-overlaying-a-badge-on-an-image.question: I want to overlay a notification badge on the top-right corner of a product image. What's the correct overlay syntax in modern SwiftUI?
q60-overlaying-a-badge-on-an-image.referenceAnswer:
Use the trailing-closure form:

```swift
Image("product")
    .overlay(alignment: .topTrailing) {
        BadgeView()
    }
```
q60-overlaying-a-badge-on-an-image.pitfall: Using the deprecated `.overlay(SomeView(), alignment:)` syntax where the view is the first argument.

q61-sharing-mutable-state-with-a-child-v.title: Sharing Mutable State with a Child View
q61-sharing-mutable-state-with-a-child-v.question: I declared `@State var count = 0` in my view. A child view also needs to mutate it — should the child also declare `@State var count`?
q61-sharing-mutable-state-with-a-child-v.pitfall: Declaring `@State` in multiple views for the same data, creating independent copies.

q62-setting-up-swiftui-previews.title: Setting Up SwiftUI Previews
q62-setting-up-swiftui-previews.question: I need to set up previews for my view. What's the modern preview syntax?
q62-setting-up-swiftui-previews.pitfall: Using the legacy `PreviewProvider` protocol with `static var previews`.

q63-menu-with-only-an-icon-label.title: Menu with Only an Icon Label
q63-menu-with-only-an-icon-label.question: I'm creating a `Menu` with just an SF Symbol icon as its label. Is that sufficient?
q63-menu-with-only-an-icon-label.referenceAnswer:
No — include a text label:

```swift
Menu("Options", systemImage: "ellipsis.circle") {
    Button("Edit", action: edit)
    Button("Delete", role: .destructive, action: delete)
}
```

The text label is used by VoiceOver even if only the icon is displayed.
q63-menu-with-only-an-icon-label.pitfall: Icon-only Menu labels, invisible to assistive technology.

q64-color-only-status-indicators.title: Color-Only Status Indicators
q64-color-only-status-indicators.question: I'm using colored circles to indicate status: red for error, yellow for warning, green for OK. Is this enough?
q64-color-only-status-indicators.pitfall: Using only color to convey meaning — unusable for colorblind users.

q65-type-safe-asset-catalog-references.title: Type-Safe Asset Catalog References
q65-type-safe-asset-catalog-references.question: I'm loading images from my asset catalog using string names. Is there a safer way to reference assets that catches errors at compile time?
q65-type-safe-asset-catalog-references.referenceAnswer:
Use generated asset symbols:

```swift
Image(.avatar)  // instead of Image("avatar")
```

This provides compile-time safety — errors surface if the asset is renamed or deleted.
q65-type-safe-asset-catalog-references.pitfall: String-based `Image("avatar")` which fails silently at runtime.

q66-accepting-child-content-in-a-custom-.title: Accepting Child Content in a Custom View
q66-accepting-child-content-in-a-custom-.question: I'm building a `CardView<Content: View>` that accepts child content via a `let content: () -> Content` closure property. Is this the right way?
q66-accepting-child-content-in-a-custom-.pitfall: Storing `() -> Content` as an escaping closure, which has overhead and is called repeatedly.

q67-parsing-an-iso-8601-date-string.title: Parsing an ISO 8601 Date String
q67-parsing-an-iso-8601-date-string.question: I have an ISO 8601 date string from an API that I need to convert to a `Date`. What's the cleanest approach?
q67-parsing-an-iso-8601-date-string.referenceAnswer:
Use the modern `Date` initializer with a parse strategy:

```swift
let date = try Date("2026-04-07T12:00:00Z", strategy: .iso8601)
```
q67-parsing-an-iso-8601-date-string.pitfall: Creating an `ISO8601DateFormatter` instance when a one-line initializer exists.

q68-handling-errors-from-user-actions.title: Handling Errors from User Actions
q68-handling-errors-from-user-actions.question: In my `catch` block, I'm calling `print(error.localizedDescription)` to log the error. Is there anything else I should do?
q68-handling-errors-from-user-actions.pitfall: Using `print()` as the only error handling for user-initiated actions.

q69-missing-combine-at-runtime.title: Missing Combine at Runtime
q69-missing-combine-at-runtime.question: I'm using a Combine publisher for debouncing in my ObservableObject, but I'm getting a runtime error about missing Combine. What's wrong?

q70-subtle-text-hierarchy-with-transpare.title: Subtle Text Hierarchy with Transparency
q70-subtle-text-hierarchy-with-transpare.question: I want my subtitle text to be slightly transparent to create visual hierarchy. Should I use `.opacity(0.6)` on the Text?
q70-subtle-text-hierarchy-with-transpare.referenceAnswer:
Use hierarchical styles which adapt to context automatically:

```swift
Text("Subtitle")
    .foregroundStyle(.secondary)
```

Available: `.primary`, `.secondary`, `.tertiary`, `.quaternary`. These adapt to light/dark mode and accessibility settings.
q70-subtle-text-hierarchy-with-transpare.pitfall: Using `.opacity(0.6)` which doesn't adapt to different appearances.

q71-where-to-attach-an-accessibility-lab.title: Where to Attach an Accessibility Label
q71-where-to-attach-an-accessibility-lab.question: Can I add a hidden `accessibilityLabel` to the Image inside my button instead of labeling the button itself?
q71-where-to-attach-an-accessibility-lab.referenceAnswer: No. The accessibility label should be attached to the interactive element — the button — not the decorative image inside it. Use `Button("Delete", systemImage: "trash", action: delete)` which provides the label to the button itself. If you label only the image, VoiceOver may not associate the label with the tappable control.
q71-where-to-attach-an-accessibility-lab.pitfall: Attaching accessibility labels to decorative child views instead of the interactive parent.

q72-hiding-an-interactive-element-from-v.title: Hiding an Interactive Element from VoiceOver
q72-hiding-an-interactive-element-from-v.question: Is it okay to use `accessibilityHidden(true)` on an interactive control to stop VoiceOver from reading it?
q72-hiding-an-interactive-element-from-v.referenceAnswer: Never hide interactive elements from VoiceOver. This makes the control completely invisible to users who rely on assistive technology. Instead, provide a proper text label. If VoiceOver reads something unexpected, fix the label rather than hiding the element.
q72-hiding-an-interactive-element-from-v.pitfall: Using `accessibilityHidden(true)` on buttons or other interactive controls.

q73-mixing-old-and-new-navigation-apis.title: Mixing Old and New Navigation APIs
q73-mixing-old-and-new-navigation-apis.question: I'm migrating an older screen to SwiftUI's newer navigation model. What migration path should I follow?
q73-mixing-old-and-new-navigation-apis.referenceAnswer: Replace `NavigationView` entirely with `NavigationStack` (or `NavigationSplitView` where the layout is multi-column). Nesting the old and new containers causes undefined behavior, double navigation bars, and broken push/pop animations. The newer navigation APIs are the replacement, not a supplement.
q73-mixing-old-and-new-navigation-apis.pitfall: Wrapping `NavigationStack` inside `NavigationView` during an incremental migration.

q74-heavy-computation-in-a-view-body.title: Heavy Computation in a View Body
q74-heavy-computation-in-a-view-body.question: Is it okay to do expensive work like JSON parsing in the view body, since SwiftUI caches view values between renders?
q74-heavy-computation-in-a-view-body.pitfall: Assuming SwiftUI caches body computations. It doesn't — body runs on every state change.

q75-calling-async-code-from-a-button.title: Calling Async Code from a Button
q75-calling-async-code-from-a-button.question: What's the best way to start async work from a button tap?
q75-calling-async-code-from-a-button.pitfall: Attempting to use `await` in a synchronous button closure.

q76-bringing-liquid-glass-into-an-app.title: Bringing Liquid Glass into an App
q76-bringing-liquid-glass-into-an-app.question: How can I bring the new Liquid Glass look into my app without making everything muddy? I'm trying to understand what SwiftUI gives me automatically, what I need to do for custom views, and what to watch for with performance.
q76-bringing-liquid-glass-into-an-app.referenceAnswer: Start by letting system components carry as much of the effect as possible — toolbars and standard controls already pick up the system's Liquid Glass styling in the right contexts. For custom elements, use `glassEffect(_:in:)` and choose the shape, tint, and interactivity that fit the component. When multiple glass elements should blend or morph together, put them in a `GlassEffectContainer`, and use `glassEffectID(_:in:)` or `glassEffectUnion(id:namespace:)` when you need coordinated transitions or a unified capsule. If a hero image needs to blur and extend under a sidebar or inspector, use `backgroundExtensionEffect()` on the image layer instead of the whole card. Keep the effect focused: too many separate glass containers or too many onscreen glass effects will hurt rendering performance.
q76-bringing-liquid-glass-into-an-app.pitfall: Sprinkling Liquid Glass across every layer, or using separate containers when a group of controls should blend and animate together.

q77-structuring-navigation-for-a-multi-s.title: Structuring Navigation for a Multi-Screen App
q77-structuring-navigation-for-a-multi-s.question: I'm building a SwiftUI app with tabs, drill-down detail screens, and some deep links. How should I structure navigation so it scales cleanly across iPhone, iPad, and Mac?
q77-structuring-navigation-for-a-multi-s.referenceAnswer: Use the modern navigation stack end-to-end. Put top-level sections in `TabView` when tabs make sense, use `NavigationStack` for push-style flows, and move to `NavigationSplitView` for multi-column layouts on larger screens. Prefer value-based `NavigationLink` plus `navigationDestination(for:)` so navigation state stays data-driven. Reach for `NavigationPath` when you need programmatic navigation, deep-link restoration, or more flexible serialized state. Keep one navigation model per flow instead of mixing old `NavigationView` patterns with new stack/split APIs.
q77-structuring-navigation-for-a-multi-s.pitfall: Mixing deprecated and modern navigation APIs, or encoding navigation state ad hoc instead of using value-based destinations and paths.

q78-designing-search-for-a-larger-swiftu.title: Designing Search for a Larger SwiftUI App
q78-designing-search-for-a-larger-swiftu.question: I need search in a larger SwiftUI app with split views and a few different result types. How do placement, scopes, suggestions, and programmatic control fit together?
q78-designing-search-for-a-larger-swiftu.referenceAnswer: Start by deciding which container or column owns search, then apply `searchable` there — attaching it to the whole `NavigationSplitView` gives automatic placement, while attaching it to a specific column makes the ownership explicit. Treat `SearchFieldPlacement` as a hint rather than a guarantee, because placement changes with platform and collapsed layouts. Layer in `searchScopes`, `searchSuggestions`, and `searchCompletion` when the search experience needs filtering or guided discovery. Use environment values like `isSearching` and `dismissSearch`, plus search focus APIs, when you need to reveal, focus, or dismiss the field programmatically. The prompt text should clarify what the search actually covers when the app has multiple result domains.
q78-designing-search-for-a-larger-swiftu.pitfall: Assuming one `searchable` placement behaves identically across macOS, iPad, and collapsed navigation states.

q79-choosing-container-views-for-a-compl.title: Choosing Container Views for a Complex Screen
q79-choosing-container-views-for-a-compl.question: I'm building a screen that mixes settings-style controls, long scrolling sections, and some grid-like content. How do I choose between stacks, lazy stacks, lists, forms, and grids?
q79-choosing-container-views-for-a-compl.referenceAnswer: Use stacks as the basic layout tool, and only move to lazy stacks when profiling shows a real performance win for large scrollable content. Choose `List` when you want system-standard collection behavior like row affordances, edit actions, and lazy loading, and choose `Form` for settings or data-entry surfaces that should adopt platform conventions automatically. Use grids when the content is genuinely two-dimensional or should scale up more naturally on larger devices. For highly custom scrolling experiences, combine `ScrollView` with lazy stacks or grids rather than forcing everything through `List`. In general, let the container express the interaction model, not only the visual arrangement.
q79-choosing-container-views-for-a-compl.pitfall: Defaulting to one container everywhere — especially hand-rolling settings screens in generic stacks or using lazy containers before measuring whether they help.

q80-making-a-custom-interface-accessible.title: Making a Custom Interface Accessible
q80-making-a-custom-interface-accessible.question: I have a custom SwiftUI interface that looks good visually, but I want it to work well with VoiceOver, keyboard focus, and other accessibility navigation. What should I be thinking about?
q80-making-a-custom-interface-accessible.referenceAnswer: Start with the basics: interactive elements need clear labels, values, and traits on the control itself, not on decorative children. Then make focus behavior intentional with APIs like `focusable`, `FocusState`, `defaultFocus`, and focused values/objects when keyboard or scene focus matters. Use grouping, headings, and sort priority to make reading order predictable, and add custom rotors or linked groups when people need to jump across structured or off-screen content. For status and animation, also respect environment settings like Reduce Motion and Differentiate Without Color so important meaning is not carried only by motion or color. Accessibility in SwiftUI is as much about navigation structure as it is about labels.
q80-making-a-custom-interface-accessible.pitfall: Treating accessibility as a layer of labels added at the end, while leaving focus order, grouping, and alternate navigation paths undefined.

q81-structuring-a-document-based-cross-p.title: Structuring a Document-Based Cross-Platform App
q81-structuring-a-document-based-cross-p.question: If I were building a document-based SwiftUI app today, what should the top-level app structure look like across document windows, shared app state, and settings?
q81-structuring-a-document-based-cross-p.referenceAnswer: Define a single `@main App` entry point and compose the app's scenes explicitly. Use `DocumentGroup` for document windows, add `Settings` for app preferences where appropriate, and add other scenes like `WindowGroup` only for non-document flows. Pick the document model that matches the app: `FileDocument`, `ReferenceFileDocument`, or the SwiftData-oriented document initializers when the model lives there. Keep truly shared app state at the app level and inject it into scenes, while letting each document window own its own document instance. If you want a polished launch experience, use the document-launch APIs rather than baking launch behavior into a regular content window.
q81-structuring-a-document-based-cross-p.pitfall: Treating document windows like ordinary app windows, which leads to the wrong scene structure and duplicated or misplaced shared state.

q82-bridging-uikit-or-appkit-into-swiftu.title: Bridging UIKit or AppKit into SwiftUI
q82-bridging-uikit-or-appkit-into-swiftu.question: I need to wrap an existing UIKit or AppKit control inside SwiftUI. How do I do that without fighting layout, update cycles, or accessibility?
q82-bridging-uikit-or-appkit-into-swiftu.referenceAnswer: Use the bridge that matches the direction of integration. When native views or view controllers need to live inside SwiftUI, use representable wrappers and keep state flowing from SwiftUI into the wrapped object through the make/update lifecycle. Add a coordinator when you need delegates, target-action callbacks, or other imperative plumbing. When SwiftUI needs to live inside an existing UIKit or AppKit surface, use hosting controllers or hosting configuration APIs so sizing and environment propagation stay consistent. In both directions, let the framework own sizing as much as possible and make sure accessibility labels, values, and semantics are surfaced at the interactive boundary instead of getting lost inside the wrapped control.
q82-bridging-uikit-or-appkit-into-swiftu.pitfall: Manually forcing frames and one-off callbacks around the wrapped control instead of using representables, coordinators, and hosting APIs as the bridge.

q83-building-a-native-menu-bar-for-a-mac.title: Building a Native Menu Bar for a Mac App
q83-building-a-native-menu-bar-for-a-mac.question: I'm building a Mac app in SwiftUI. What's the right way to add app-specific menu bar commands, keyboard shortcuts, and dynamic menu state without rebuilding the system menus from scratch?
q83-building-a-native-menu-bar-for-a-mac.referenceAnswer: Let your scenes provide the default menu structure, then extend it with the commands system. Attach `.commands` to the relevant scene, use `CommandMenu` for top-level app-specific menus, and use `CommandGroup` when you want to insert, replace, or extend standard system menu groups. Pull in built-in command sets like `SidebarCommands`, `ToolbarCommands`, or text-formatting commands when the app supports those capabilities, rather than recreating them manually. Add `keyboardShortcut` where it improves discoverability, and use focused values or other scene-aware state so menu titles and enablement follow the active window and selection.
q83-building-a-native-menu-bar-for-a-mac.pitfall: Treating the menu bar like a pile of custom buttons, or replacing standard command groups when the app really just needs to extend them.
